data.title: Ross Stores: Q3 Strength Confirms This Retailer Still Knows How To Win
data.description:
When I went to the US earlier this year, I visited two Ross Stores (ROST). It's a business model that I find really interesting, and there's nothing like it here in Brazil. The appeal of it being practically a treasure hunt really works, creating that feeling that you'll eventually find a product you like at a really low price.

And what makes this possible are precisely the moats around the business model that the company has managed to build. It is a large network of merchants and stores, which is managed very efficiently with what the company calls “disciplined expense & inventory management.”

Q3 proved this, with the ROST stock rising 8.4%. But still, I prefer to be more conservative and buy when there is a better entry point, i.e., when the valuation is slightly better.

Q3 Earnings: Pretty Solid

Ross Stores' Q3 earnings were strong. The company surprised market expectations by $190 million in terms of revenue, and EPS was even more surprising, with Ross delivering $1.58, a beat of $0.16, or a surprise of more than 10%.

The guidance was also positive, with an outlook of $6.42 (midpoint) for 2025, while the market expected something closer to $6.25.

But in my view, the main thing was the execution in this quarter, which only proves that the business is strong and that management has vision (and in the long run, this matters much more to shareholder value than any beat or miss). In Q3, the company achieved comparable store sales of 7%. This is very strong; over the last 9 months, this indicator has been at 3%, and the guidance for Q4 is also close to this.

Having comparable store sales of 7% in a quarter that was not so easy for the economy, with many retail players struggling to maintain low single-digit growth, shows the strength of Ross Stores. In the earnings call, they attributed this strong performance to “compelling brand name assortments” and “new marketing campaigns.”

The operating margin for the quarter was 11.5%, with operating income of $648 million, impacted by tariffs and higher distribution costs, but offset by lower domestic freight and occupancy costs. Even with this very dynamic Q3, the operating margin improved, as it was just under ~10% in Q3 2024.

In short, I interpret ROST’s Q3 as a pretty solid quarter, both in terms of financials and outlook, which point to a solid business and gradual expansion.

ROST Valuation: Not Great

Although there are some risks, such as higher tariff costs that may occur in the coming quarters or poor execution in any quarter, like a worse inventory management (but management mentioned that it is healthy for the holiday season), the main risk to ROST stock, in my view, is its valuation.

It is not a bad valuation, but there is also no attractive margin of safety.

At first glance, ROST's valuation looks good. The company trades at a P/E of 25x, but as it maintains EPS growth and reliable prospects, it is not such a stretched multiple. Compared to its main peer, TJX Companies (TJX), these multiples also seem much more acceptable. Note how ROST is cheaper on virtually all metrics, even though it has similar growth.

But what bothered me the most was that in its presentation, the company shows a “potential” for 3,600 stores, versus the current ~2,200 (proxy), not that much if we run the numbers and think this may be a “full maturity.” But this also makes it easier to envision where Ross can be in the next 10 years or so.

If it continues at this pace, Ross might reach 3,600 stores by something near 2035. If revenue per store grows 3% per year, which is feasible considering inflation and some initiatives, we are talking about revenue of ~$50 billion by 2035. With a net margin of 10%, slightly higher than what it is delivering today, Ross would be trading at a P/E ratio of around 11x in 2035.

It really is a cheap multiple, as it would allow a dividend yield of 9% (on cost) if the company distributed all of this net income. Assuming that the exit multiple is 25x, we are talking about an upside of 123% in this period, but that in 10 years is about 8% per year. Another point is that I am not convinced that 25x earnings for Ross in 2035 is reasonable. On the one hand, it will be a more mature and established company, but at the same time, there will also be less room for expansion, and perhaps the market will want to pay less for the lower potential.

In other words, even if this scenario occurs, we are talking about only a reasonable return, around 8% per year or a little more depending on the premise.

The Bottom Line

Ross Stores is a strong company, and I like its business model and execution, which is why I believe it is a potential compounder if we are talking about the very long term. Still, it is not at a price that I consider attractive enough to initiate a position. Therefore, my initial coverage of ROST stock is a hold.

data.title: Eli Lilly: The Weight-Loss Leader With More Upside If Execution Holds
data.description:
Following my last article on Eli Lilly (LLY), the stock price surged by 47% and significantly outperformed the benchmark. I had a Strong Buy rating with a $800 price target, which was exceeded, and the company currently trades around its all-time high of roughly $1,030.

As I have anticipated, the obesity treatment market remained the key top and bottom-line driver. The company continues to be a pharma powerhouse in the weight-loss landscape, fueling a bullish narrative over the following years.

As volatility returned to the broader markets, Eli Lilly presented stability in business performance and a semi-defensive approach tied to the health landscape, which remains people's core priority. Therefore, LLY may appear to be a compelling diversification candidate, helping withstand market turbulence.

The healthcare companies of the S&P500 are still trading at a multi-year low valuation, highlighting an 18.40x earnings multiple, and I think a positive shift in sentiment may spark an end-of-year rally for the industry. If that happens, Eli Lilly appears to be well-positioned to benefit from improved sentiment. Therefore, my bullish thesis remains unchanged. I rate Eli Lilly a Strong Buy with a $1,165 price target over the next 12 months, highlighting a 14% upside possibility and broader market outperformance.

FQ3 2025: Phenomenal Quarter, Highlighting Eli Lilly's Dominance

On October 30, the healthcare powerhouse delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was an outstanding quarterly performance, highlighting accelerated top and bottom-line growth. The market reacted to the results with a 4% stock price appreciation.

Eli Lilly posted $17.6 billion in revenue, representing a 54% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $1.53 billion above analysts' estimates. The bottom line arrived at $7.02 in diluted EPS, reflecting a 5x surge over the past 12 months, and $1.13 above what the market had anticipated. If the company can continue delivering top and bottom-line growth at a high double-digit pace, I think the market may be willing to pay a premium for the company further.

In the earnings presentation, the healthcare powerhouse indicated that top-line growth was mainly driven by key products, representing $12 billion in revenue, highlighting a 104% surge. This portfolio consists of Mounjaro and Zepbound, weight-loss blockbusters, Kisunla, used for Alzheimer's, the cancer treatment drug Jaypirca, and others. This suggests that Eli Lilly remains a well-positioned healthcare powerhouse. A triple-digit revenue growth in the core portfolio is phenomenal and argues for the company's future success.

In addition to this, management emphasized its Kisunla drug has been approved in the EU, and Inluriyo, used for treating advanced or metastatic breast cancer, has been approved in the US, suggesting a positive tailwind and success in the anticipated launches. The company highlighted study results, indicating prolonged survival rates for their Vezenio, an early breast cancer drug, Jaypirca achieved a substantial improvement in progression-free survival in CLL/SLL treatment. The Orforglipron experienced successful results in the second obesity trial, and LLY has proceeded with a global regulatory submission in 2025.

This indicates that Eli Lilly remains a high-quality company, expanding its pipeline of innovations, which fuels a bullish narrative. The company posted $3.5 billion in research and development expenses, suggesting a 27% growth on a year-over-year basis, implying LLY positions itself to continue dominating the market over the following years.

In the press release, Eli Lilly management indicated that the US was the main region driving the top-line growth. The company posted $11.3 billion in revenue, highlighting a 45% increase over the past 12 months. This accounts for 64% of the company's total revenue. The revenue from international markets surged by 74% on a year-over-year basis, reaching $6.3 billion. Management indicated that Mounjaro was the main contributor outside the US.

Although it seems that Eli Lilly remains heavily dependent on the revenue derived from the US, international markets are gaining strong momentum. If the company can sustain such high double-digit international revenue growth further, it would become an even more diversified business, fueling a bullish narrative in the following quarters.

The company's diabetes and obesity treatment drugs continue delivering significant performance in the US. Mounjaro experienced a market share increase in both new-to-brand prescriptions and total prescriptions in the type 2 diabetes space, indicating a rise of 4 bps on a year-over-year basis. This highlights a 54% surge in NBRx and 45% growth in terms of Trx in the US, respectively. As I have indicated above, Mounjaro led top-line contribution internationally, signaling a 54% growth outside the US, which suggests that LLY positions itself to become a more globally diversified healthcare powerhouse.

The weight-loss blockbuster Zepbound continues its dominance in the US. The anti-obesity drug achieved a 63% market share in the US in FQ3 2025. Although this has represented a slight decrease by 2 bps, there are no structural issues, and LLY continues to dominate the weight-loss landscape. To support this, management revealed that Lilly's market share surged to 57.9% in the US. Furthermore, the global obesity treatment market was valued at roughly $16 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to quadruple by 2030, reaching $60 billion. This represents a 22.31% CAGR, and if LLY maintains a dominant stance, it will remain one of the key beneficiaries of the broader market growth.

The company continues a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends and a share repurchase program. Eli Lilly returned $1.3 billion to its shareholders in dividends and $700 million in share buybacks. If the company continues such a shareholder-friendly capital strategy, the share repurchase program may support a significant double-digit bottom-line increase.

The healthace powerhouse achieved 82.9% gross profit margin in the quarter, which suggests significant fundamentals and outstanding business performance. The operating margin was 41.8% in FQ3 2025. I think $900 billion market capitalization company operating at such outstanding margins, and in parallel delivering a substantial top-line growth, deserves a premium valuation.

To conclude, this has been a phenomenal quarter for the pharma heavyweight. The company's key portfolio is driving both top and bottom growth, and dominates respective markets. Eli Lilly is growing revenue at a rapid pace in both the US and international markets, making it a compelling, well-diversified opportunity. In addition, LLY operates at premium margins, supporting a bullish narrative and positive outlook in the following years. I think it may seem to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors seeking pharma exposure.

Although Eli Lilly Trades At A Premium, It May Be Cheap If Growth Delivers

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, highlighting a more in-depth valuation analysis of the company. Regardless, I would also like to note a few things.

Eli Lilly trades at a forward P/E of approximately 44, representing a 2.3x premium versus peers at 18.81x. The company is also trading at roughly an 80% premium versus the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23x earnings multiple. Although this may appear significant, the pharma powerhouse delivered a high double-digit revenue growth and triple-digit bottom-line growth in the last quarter; therefore, I argue the stock may be cheap if growth delivers.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 1.37, Eli Lilly is actually trading at a discount to peers at 1.77. This indicates a 23% undervaluation and supports my bullish thesis arguing for multiple expansion.

However, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 15.05 and 14.54, respectively, offset the bullish outlook and suggest a premium valuation. These valuation metrics reveal a 3.5x overvaluation at the midpoint versus the sector medians of 3.80 and 4.09. This signals that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth; therefore, even if LLY continues to deliver such phenomenal top-line growth, there is a chance it will not translate into meaningful stock price appreciation. On the other hand, it could also indicate that investors are willing to pay a substantial premium for the company's business and dominant market position.

Over the past 12 months, Eli Lilly posted 45% revenue growth. This implies a 7.5x outperformance versus peers at 6% and a 4.5x outperformance versus the benchmark at a historical 10% top-line expansion. LLY delivered 121% bottom-line growth, and that represents a massive outperformance versus the sector median of 17%, and 12x outperformance versus the average company in the S&P 500 at approximately 10% bottom-line growth.

Therefore, although the company delivers a significantly more rapid top and bottom line growth, it trades at roughly 2x premium versus both the industry and the benchmark. I think this supports my thesis that Eli Lilly may actually remain cheap if it continues to perform at such robust levels.

I kindly invite you to read my previous article on the LLY stock. Nevertheless, to conclude, Eli Lilly remains a phenomenal top- and bottom-line growth story, justified by massive demand and market dominance. Despite rapidly growing business fundamentals, Eli Lilly maintains elevated margins, validated by gross profit margin (TTM) of 83% and net income margin (TTM) of 31%. These metrics argue for a bullish narrative.

In addition, the pharma company is tied to the obesity treatment market tailwinds, and now, with the drug pricing agreement reached with the US, Eli Lilly has reduced uncertainty tied to tariffs and their potential impact. Although the deal may affect margins in the short term, LLY has pricing power; therefore, it should not cause structural risks. I believe Eli Lilly remains a high-quality, dominant player in the operating landscape, and it may appear to be a compelling diversification candidate.

Wall Street is estimating $23.68 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $32.11 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I agree with the market's expectations, as I believe Eli Lilly remains well-positioned to achieve these targets. I also believe the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line performance in the following quarters.

Although I believe Eli Lilly deserves a significantly higher earnings multiple, I favor a more conservative approach. Therefore, if we apply a forward P/E of about 30 to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $32.11, we arrive at my price target of $1,165. I think the strong momentum in demand may continue further, and that should fuel top- and bottom-line growth.

Eli Lilly Is A Healthcare Powerhouse, And My Bullish Thesis Remains Intact

I think Eli Lilly remains a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking diversification into the healthcare sector. The industry trades at multi-year lows; thus, once sentiment improves, it could act as a tailwind for LLY. Although the stock trades at a premium, based on my analysis, it could actually be cheap if growth delivers.

The risks and concerns remain as highlighted above, and in my previous article. Eli Lilly remains a moderately leveraged business with $42.58 billion in total obligations, and that could partially cloud the outlook. The company remains heavily dependent on GLP-1 blockbusters' performance, and sentiment may shift if demand weakens. Nevertheless, I argue that tailwinds outweigh the headwinds, and Eli Lilly remains well-positioned to dominate the pharma space and benefit from broader market growth.

Therefore, my bullish thesis remains intact. I rate Eli Lilly a Strong Buy with an increased price target of $1,165 over the next 12 months. This reflects a 14% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 30 Wall Street analysts imply a $1,012 price target over the following year, indicating a nearly 4% downside potential. Although this is definitely a possibility, I think the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line performance, and if a rapid pace is sustained, it may appreciate. This is justified by management's excellent execution, strong market position, solid momentum in demand, and tailwinds tied to its future growth. Time will tell, but it will be interesting to monitor if projections play out.

data.title: Meta: Good Time To Buy The Social Media Winner
data.description:
Introduction

Meta Platforms (META) is one of the interesting cases that shows AI has not yet played a significant role in the secular growth of domain businesses. The company's stock price has been punished for what appears to be a likely perceived AI weakness, as well as the so-called AI big capex spending. The recent Meta quarterly report shows Meta has grown revenue by around 26% while keeping the high gross margin of over 83%. These numbers have been achieved with all the "AI misses" and are much better than those of AI leaders (achievers) like Google and Microsoft, etc., as we'll discuss in more detail later. The stock price has been down -21% since October 29, when the Q3 earnings were announced. I believe the stock is a good buy because Meta's still the social media giant in this secularly growing sector, evidenced by the competitors like YouTube and TikTok. AI or not, the organic growth prosperity still looks very promising and is proven once again by its Q3 report.

Meta Platforms Overview

Meta is a social media giant through its several major platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. Its large market share and ownership of those social platforms confirm its status as a dominant force in the industry. In fact, there is an antitrust monopoly lawsuit filed against Meta for the same reason. It is interesting to note that the lawsuit has been ruled in favor of Meta based on the company's claim of "declining in popularity" amid the formidable competition, such as TikTok and YouTube.

However, META's recent Q3 report does not show any signs of "declining". The organic growth of the social media relevant to Meta's core business has grown at a respectable rate, as summarized below:

Keep in mind that the revenue growth is at 26%, reaching $51 billion per quarter. The growth rate is much higher than the 5-year average (19%), as shown below.

On the other hand, the gross margin remains higher than 80%, which is a strong foundation to keep the company highly profitable. The operating margin was 40.1% in Q3 2025, a decrease from 42.7% one year ago, due to increased costs and expenses such as AI investments.

The company's stock price has suffered a big loss of over 21% post Q3 earnings, as shown below

One of the reasons to blame is that the reported GAAP net income ($2.71 billion) was an 83% year-over-year decrease, significantly impacted by a one-time, non-cash tax charge of $15.93 billion. Excluding the charge, non-GAAP net income would have been $18.64 billion, more in line with previous quarters. I believe this charge has no impact on the plans for the future, such as revenues and earnings. Any related effect on the stock price could be short-lived.

The bullish earnings cycle remains resilient.

META is considered one of the hyperscalers, which typically have billions of users. META's powerful vision targeting "human connection" is probably covering the biggest potential market amongst billions of consumers. I think the real moat for META resides in its applications, which are truly hyper-scaler, including the following data reported recently:

  • 3.5 billion people use at least one of the apps daily.
  • Instagram features 3 billion monthly active users.
  • Threads (a text-based social app) has over 150 million daily active users.
  • Video time on Instagram has grown more than 30% since last year.
  • Reels (a short-form video) sees an annual run rate of over $50 billion.

With one of the best product management teams and the founder-led execution environment, I don't really see a real chance of anyone taking over in the next decade or so in the natural course of the evolution for META's envisioned "people connection".

Notice that the impressive growth reported amid AI struggling (more to be discussed in the next section) has implied that the remarkable topline growth is really organic to its core businesses.

AI Capex Spending Myth - Where is the next innovation

Meta AI seems to face some product challenges, including a lack of catalyst for the competitive AI products, and ethical and safety concerns surrounding its chatbots. Difficulty in retaining top AI talent could also bring some uncertainties for its future AI. I have been looking forward to seeing a new and exciting AI roadmap from META for quite some time, but I will have to wait a little more time before it happens.

Despite such perceived AI weakness compared to the other hyperscalers, such as Google, etc., Meta Platforms is doubling down on AI investment in the compute infrastructure and next-generation engagement products. In fact, META has been raising its CapEx and expense outlook for both 2025 and 2026.

One of my favorite indicators for technology growth is the R&D (research and development) spending. A significant portion of AI spending is likely considered R&D for both accounting and tax purposes. R&D is critical for a technology company to keep its technology leadership, build/keep a moat, and facilitate the rapid cycle and not be disrupted.

I want to see META continue to raise AI spending, which could lead to a potential AI growth catalyst in the future. Just like what the CEO Zuckerberg stated in the Q3 earnings conference, that

"I am very focused on establishing Meta as the leading frontier AI lab. We're heads down developing our next generation of models and products, and I'm looking forward to sharing more on that front over the coming months."

I don't see much difference from the other hyperscalers in terms of AI visions, plans, and, more importantly, the spending. On the other hand, I could imagine the stock would sell off more if any of the hyperscalers were an outlier to announce a cut in the AI capex spending instead. AI can play the other way and crush the stock price like a bubble burst if AI gets "hyped" too much and does not see the expected outcome.

A safer Mag-7 stock to own

Meta's stock is selling off after its recent earnings report due to a combination of a large, one-time tax charge and concerns about the company's "aggressive" and significantly increased capital expenditure for artificial intelligence (AI) in 2026.

With all the negative sentiment built into META and the -21% price drop, the META stock may be less vulnerable to an AI bubble burst than the other current AI leaders, like Microsoft and Google. In other words, META would already be priced in a good "AI correction," and the AI fiction has already been largely removed. As discussed earlier, the AI could be becoming the tool for META to figure out the next-gen products, which will turn the AI spending into a tailwind instead of part of the walls of worry (e.g., alarm bells, etc.) about the AI capex spending for the hyperscalers.

On the other hand, META has now become a very attractive growth play amongst the mega-cap names. Below shows some of the metrics illustrating that META has the highest margin, highest revenue (market) growth, and lowest PEG ratio.

I don't see any reason META should be priced this cheaply based on the organic growth mentioned earlier. In fact, the higher margin with high growth is the right recipe for the multiple expansion and rewarding a much higher valuation. I see a potential price recovery of about 20% bringing META to the "fair valuation" of such a peer group.

The following is the earnings estimate for the next few years. Based on a similar growth trajectory, META would see PE in its teens in 2026.

Buffett has recently bought a chunk of Google shares, accounting for about 1.3% of Berkshire's portfolio. Now, based on the comparisons shown above, Google appears to be much more expensive than META. I think META is a better BUY at this level. I have opened a significant position recently and expect META may see a good rebound if we have a traditional year-end rally in the market.

Risk Analysis

The AI growth scare may continue to have an impact on the META stock price in the short term. There is also a risk that the multibillion dollar capex spending may not bring the AI or organic growth as discussed above to fruition. As a result, the growth projection would likely be cut and cause the stock to go even lower.

The regulation challenges have always remained a key issue in recent years. There is no guarantee the situation will be improved in the future. In other words, there could be a probability of hitting big negative news on that front, and making the stock price highly volatile.

Closing

The recent big drop in the META stock price does not seem to stem from worries about its future growth. The reported big write-off has no impact on the balance sheet, which remains strong. Meta's increased capex spending is a must-have R&D spending for future growth in the technology world, regardless of AI or whatever will come in the future. I see a big buying opportunity for META for the long-term investment, as the stock is in a deep oversold situation with RSI reaching well below 30 right now. We may see a possible price rebound should the bull market sentiment continue in the rest of 2025.

data.title: Celestica: The Overlooked Cisco Parallel
data.description:
The fact that Celestica's (CLS) share price has increased by 60% since my previous bearish call means the stock has become even more overvalued. Yes, the takeaways from my previous multiples analysis did not age well. Therefore, this time I invested my time into building a DCF model with two different scenarios to demonstrate how significantly CLS is overvalued. Only extremely aggressive growth assumptions like an 8% perpetual growth rate justify Celestica's current $50 billion valuation. While history suggests that such a perpetual growth rate is not impossible, as companies like Microsoft and Apple maintain double-digit revenue growth for decades, Celestica is not a company that writes a script and creates new "Blue Oceans". The company is extremely good at capitalizing on AI tailwinds, but I think that Celestica is more likely to repeat Cisco's growth trajectory rather than Microsoft's or Apple's. Of course, Celestica might soar further in 2026 if there will be new AI infrastructure budget boosts from the largest tech companies. However, betting on it when they have already increased their CAPEX budgets to around $100 billion each is pure gambling rather than value investing. All in all, I reiterate "Strong Sell" for CLS.

CLS' massive overvaluation

CLS is up by 239% YTD, bringing the company's market cap to almost $50 billion. If we look at the valuation ratios, they are extremely high compared to the company's historical averages. I said the same in my previous CLS article and even highlighted that Celestica's valuation ratios mirrored Nvidia's. However, since my previous bearish call did not age well, I think that I must change my valuation approach this time. Therefore, I am returning to the good old DCF model that did not let me down when I found out that CLS was undervalued several quarters ago. Since total debt constitutes only around 2% of Celestica's capital structure, I think that cost of equity is a suitable metric to be used as a discount rate.

According to my calculations using the CAPM formula, Celestica's cost of equity is 10.7%. This is quite high compared to the current risk-free rate, but I think it is fair due to the stock's extremely high volatility, with the last 52-week share price range spanning from $83 to $512.

After the discount rate is figured out, we can proceed with other DCF assumptions. Let's start with the one with the highest certainty - the free cash flow [FCF]. According to the latest earnings call, the management's FCF guidance for FY2025 is $425 million [the most optimistic scenario is used to challenge my overvaluation claims]. Since we are calculating the fair value for the next twelve months, the base-year is FY2026. Therefore, we need to adjust the $425 million guidance. Since CLS demonstrates a solid correlation between EPS and FCF growth due to AI tailwinds, let's inflate the FY2025 FCF by Wall Street's EPS growth forecast for FY2026, which is 41.3%. As a result, the base-year FCF assumption is rounded to $600 million [$425 million*1.41].

First of all, I wanted to show a scenario with growth assumptions that justify Celestica's current $50 billion market cap. For the company's fair value to reach $50 billion in the DCF model, I applied a 25% FCF CAGR for the seven-year period between FY2026 and FY2032 and an 8% perpetual growth rate.

In general, the above table shows us that these growth rates are not impossible. Nvidia has been an absolute champion over the last 30 years with almost a 50% revenue CAGR since 1995, but it is mostly due to almost zero revenue in the base year. Nevertheless, companies like Microsoft and Apple show that a double-digit growth rate over three decades is not impossible. Of course, three decades is not perpetuity, but these companies are still driving revenue growth. For example, Microsoft is expected to maintain double-digit revenue growth over the next decade.

The biggest question here is how realistic these growth assumptions are for a company like Celestica. I think that they are unrealistic for Celestica precisely because companies like Microsoft and Apple have been writing the script of the modern world and created multiple blue oceans throughout their histories. With all due respect, Celestica is not writing the script and is not creating blue oceans. All these switches and other hardware for data centers that Celestica offers were around before Celestica. They were good at enhancing the performance of their products over the last few years, but they were not the ones who first invented hardware for data centers. Of course, we have to give credit to Celestica because their ability to capitalize on the data center boom is exceptional.

But what happens once the power of AI tailwinds fades? Let's be fair while trying to answer this question. Celestica doesn't boast a multi-billion cash pile, and its current FCF is not measured in billions either. Therefore, it is impossible to expect a massive R&D boost or an M&A spree from them. With such a setup, it is difficult to expect that Celestica might create new "Blue Oceans" or start disrupting the entire technological landscape. Yes, companies like Apple and Microsoft were started in garages with tiny upfront investments, but these times are far in the past. The modern world is so competitive, and tech companies boast so many financial resources at the moment.

Even Cisco, the biggest darling of the dot-com era that had billions in profits and resources to reinvest in growth, delivered a 1.5% revenue CAGR over the last decade. And even amid the AI era, their revenue growth is expected to remain in the mid-single digits. It is a clear indication that even well-positioned infrastructure players face massive growth headwinds post-boom because key income statement numbers have become too large to maintain growth.

Therefore, I urge investors not to expect a miracle from Celestica. With all that being said, let's return to the DCF model and simulate a scenario with less aggressive growth assumptions. For the second scenario, let's assume that AI tailwinds will remain robust over the next seven years and Celestica will be able to maintain a 20% FCF CAGR. For the perpetual growth rate, let's assume that CLS will be able to maintain growth in line with the U.S. GDP growth rate over the last three decades, which was 5%, according to one of my above tables.

Even with these still quite optimistic assumptions, the DCF model suggests massive overvaluation. The business's fair value is close to $21 billion, almost 2.5 times lower than the current market cap. Dividing Celestica's current share price of $335 by 2.5 times equals $134, which is my target price.

Risks to my bearishness

As I said earlier, we have to give credit to Celestica's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds. The company released its Q3 2025 earnings in late October, and it was another confident double beat against consensus estimates. YoY revenue growth accelerated for the fourth quarter in a row to almost 28%. The company's bottom line grew by far faster with a YoY increase from $1.04 to $1.58.

It was high quality EPS growth because Celestica demonstrates robust operating leverage. The company's operating margin expanded from 5.6% to 10.3% on a YoY basis, which is a massive improvement. As a result, the cash generating capacity also strengthened. As I mentioned before, the FY2025 full-year FCF guidance is $425 million compared to $306 million generated in FY2024. Hands down, absolutely brilliant quarter for Celestica.

They are doing extremely well amid the AI revolution, and the main question is how long all this data center spending spree will continue. We saw during this earnings season that all the largest AI players have increased their CAPEX budgets for both 2025 and 2026. There were concerns about the AI bubble recently, which spooked the market, but Nvidia's Q3 earnings release made these fears seem overblown.

Forecasting the duration of the data center spending spree is extremely difficult. On the one hand, despite Magnificent 7 companies boasting massive cash piles, maintaining current CAPEX budgets beyond FY2026 does not look sustainable. On the other hand, their total debt levels measured in billions while market caps are measured in trillions means that they have room to raise debt financing, which can be allocated to boost data center investments even further. The main point here is that if next year all the tech giants continue boosting their AI infrastructure spending, we will highly likely see CLS continue soaring despite overvaluation.

Bottom line

To conclude, I reiterate "Strong Sell" for Celestica because the premium over its intrinsic value is too large, even taking into account the company's stellar ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds.

data.title: VTEB: Exempt And Potential Higher Yield
data.description:
To obtain income that is exempt from federal taxes, the Vanguard Tax-Exempt Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (VTEB) may be considered a good option. This is a passively managed ETF that seeks to replicate the performance of the S&P National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index, an index that measures the performance of the investment-grade municipal bond segment. By passively replicating the index, the ETF purchases public bonds that meet two conditions: (1) they have good credit quality, and (2) the coupons on these bonds are exempt from federal income tax and the alternative minimum tax [AMT].

From my point of view, and as we will see below, I believe that now is a good time to buy this ETF as an additional component of a bond portfolio for investors with a medium- to long-term time horizon. Today, investors can lock in a federal tax-exempt yield of around 3.2%–3.3% (TTM) per annum, with an almost symbolic associated cost of 0.03% per annum (expense ratio). Another argument is that the VTEB ETF, with this tax-exempt return, can compete with other vehicles that purchase intermediate-maturity or investment-grade Treasury bonds in terms of income, assuming lower credit risk, since the municipal bonds it holds are mostly in the AA/AAA/A category.

Fund Profile

In the table above, we can see the main attributes of the ETF analyzed, which help us to understand it a little better. As of today, the fund holds 9,960 fixed-income securities belonging to agencies, states, and municipalities, which reflects its high diversification and low exposure to credit risk by issuer. We can also see that the yield to maturity is 3.9% compared to an average coupon of 4.5%, which means that many bonds are trading above par, a situation that is typical of an environment where interest rates have risen since the bonds were issued. The average duration of the ETF is 7.2 years, which categorizes it as a medium-term vehicle, somewhat sensitive to interest rate movements.

In the risk profile table provided by Vanguard on its website, we can see how they categorize the fund in the intermediate range in terms of duration (7.2 years) and the highest quality range (Treasury/Agency), due to the bonds it holds, which we will discuss below. This snapshot gives us an idea of the main risk of this ETF, which is none other than medium-term interest rate movements.

Portfolio: Issuer Type & Credit Quality

As mentioned in the previous section, the snapshot above shows the ETF's holdings broken down by credit quality. We can see how the VTEB ETF plays in the “high” end of the municipal debt universe. Currently, the ETF has around 79% of its assets in AAA or AA bonds. These tranches are followed by the A tranche with an approximate weight of 18% and the BBB tranche with 1.9%. It is an ETF with a profile of quality and stable debt.

The second image (Issuer Type) reinforces the previous idea. Ninety-nine point nine six percent of assets are in the “Other” category, i.e., municipal and quasi-municipal issuers, while exposure to Treasury/Agency is symbolic (0.04%), basically cash management. This makes VTEB an AMT-free domestic vehicle, not a hodgepodge of government and corporate bonds. This is important for portfolio construction because, on the one hand, it is precisely this mix that gives it tax exemption and, on the other hand, the correlation with Treasuries or IG credit will be high, so VTEB will provide real diversification within the fixed income block.

The image above once again highlights how diversified the vehicle is in terms of its top ten holdings. No single position accounts for more than 0.15% of the fund, and the top ten holdings barely add up to 1%. In this case, the issuers are very recognizable (University of California, State of California, Maryland, New York, and New Jersey).

Historical Returns and Sensitivity to Interest Rates

The image above shows a comparison of the performance of the fund analyzed against its benchmark or index that it replicates. As we can see, the ETF does what it promises. It replicates the performance of the index itself almost perfectly. The VTEB and benchmark bars practically overlap in all the horizons shown in the image, confirming the success of the sampling strategy they apply to replicate the index. We can also see that in the medium and long term, the carry increases due to the tax-exempt effect. In my opinion, this indicates that the vehicle rewards patient investors, which is why we said at the beginning that we recommend buying for the medium to long term.

Yield, Income Distribution, and Tax Advantage

This chart (Dividend Yield) shows us why it currently makes sense to consider buying VTEB. Over the last year, the ETF's dividend yield has moved away from the 3.0% range to reach 3.25% today. If we add to this the 30-day SEC yield published by Vanguard (currently 3.47%), we can convert the yield to a tax-equivalent yield.

For an average taxpayer, a 3.47% tax exemption is equivalent to a taxable return of 4.7%–5.4%. In this case, you would be obtaining the same return, due to the exemption, while assuming less risk than if you invested this part of the portfolio in bonds with longer maturities or lower credit quality.

The Peers

Comparing neighbors helps us put the fund's performance in context relative to its peers. As you can see in the chart, all funds follow a similar trajectory: their returns increase or decrease in almost identical fashion. The good thing about this is that we can see how VTEB achieves a performance similar to its competitors, with a much lower management fee than the rest of its comparables. The first image reflects this. VTEB has a fee of 0.03%, and the next lowest is that of the MMIT ETF at 0.30%, which is 10 times higher. The shape of the graph also tells us that it is the movement of the yield curve that is the dominant factor in this case, rather than the skill of the fund manager.

Final Thoughts

To summarize and conclude the analysis, VTEB is a good fit as a core holding for medium- to long-term investors seeking to stabilize their portfolio, improve after-tax yield, and avoid relying on an active manager or paying 20–30 basis points in additional fees for a similar strategy. It is not a solution for parking liquidity for six months or for accounts with their own tax advantages (IRA, 401(k)), but it is an efficient way to anchor the conservative part of the portfolio in an environment where, finally, municipal bonds are once again paying for the risk you take. If interest rates continue to fall in the medium term, as seems likely, you can enter now by fixing the purchase price and then take advantage of the revaluation of the bonds in the ETF portfolio when interest rates fall again in the future. With that, the yield on cost may be better today than it will be for new buyers in the future. Thank you for reading.

data.title: ISD: Competitive CEF, And A Hidden Risk To Face
data.description:
When I think of a competitive CEF in the Junk segment, I think of PGIM High Yield Bond Fund, Inc. (ISD). And I say this because it has proven to be a fund capable of competing in the post-2022 recovery well compared to other passive ETFs, but also to the other CEFs themselves. The market has appreciated its operations to such an extent that it made the discount disappear, which had instead generally characterized this CEF. And the question we are asking ourselves right now is, today, can it still be able to compete in this market? If as a technical structure I appreciate it a lot, I am quite worried about the context in which it is placed.

Let me explain myself better.

What is ISD?

ISD is a closed-end fund (CEF) managed by PGIM, focused on junk bonds (91% of the portfolio). The fund’s stated objective is to generate a high current income, and it succeeds with distributions that today exceed 8.7%. The expense ratio is 1.12%, high compared to an ETF, but not so distinctive when considering the CEF category. At the same time, it should be specified that in the bond market, certain costs cannot certainly be overlooked, considering that we are talking about lower average annual returns. It will therefore be important to understand the real impact of ISD in portfolios compared simply to 100% passive structures (such as ETFs).

To this is added the fact that, although typically traded at a discount (around -6% on average over 3 years), today we are talking about a premium of +1.27%, which indirectly can be considered a cost, even if in technical terms it is also the demonstration of strong confidence from the reference market.

Holding distribution

The composition reflects the assumptions specified in the literature. In fact, 91% junk bond, the rest divided among:

  • Treasuries
  • CLO (3.2%)
  • ETF PGIM AAA CLO (3.4%)
  • Floating-rate loans (7.3%)

Considering the potential application of leverage even above 20%, although it is not visible in the recent factsheet, there is an Average Maturity of 5.3 years and a Leverage Adjusted Duration of 3.7 years. Which makes it typically with higher duration than the average of high-yield and corporate bonds with ratings similar to the distribution of the securities inside ISD, meaning on average unbalanced toward BB (45% BB, 25% B, 11% CCC). A risk that, however, is well distributed today, considering that we are talking about more than 800 holdings, and the presence within it of other solutions in turn diversified, primarily, with a weight above 3%, PAAA (which I have already discussed here).

Likewise, ISD uses currency hedging on GBP and EUR, a rare practice for an almost entirely domestic HY CEF. Few bonds are denominated in foreign currency.

Peer table

Today it is approaching its objective of current income with a yield of 8.7%, definitely competitive compared to other bond ETFs, such as the classic AGG with a yield of 3.79%, or more in theme JNK, with a yield of 6.52%. This has in the past been amplified by the use of leverage rather than exclusively by a greater risk appetite of the underlying, an element that indeed generates more volatility in this solution compared to the mentioned ETFs.

Average 30-day volatility since inception:

  • ISD: 10.8%
  • HYG: 6.5%

Likewise, the fact that the NAV is increasing reduces concerns about capital erosion. An element that I care to verify when I analyze CEFs, especially those with high distributions like this one. In this case, here is my peer table, useful for a direct comparison.

In fact, drawing a parallel only in terms of price return, ISD seems to be the structure that has managed to maintain its capital value better. An element that moves away from the idea of NAV erosion.

Surely, one element that influenced this is the fact that it remains a fund that on average distributes less, so in theory there are also fewer possibilities of payout crises, therefore NAV erosion. A look of interest should therefore go to the total return.

And this is where an interesting piece of evidence emerges: even looking at the total return, the formula proposed by ISD has proven successful in this phase of economic expansion / contraction of credit spreads.

Not surprisingly, from reading the most recent composition, about 90% comes from income, while 10% comes from return of capital (ROC).

What moves ISD

The last 2 years have had a decidedly positive impact for ISD: first of all, the Fed cuts have reduced the NII (but very slowly), mainly on the CLO and floating-rate portion, which in total we have seen affect anyway more than 15% of the holding distribution. This has reduced the distributions.

This has not been a problem in the past for the fund, because the spread between NII and distribution is covered by the mark-to-market. In fact, this has had a positive impact in terms of price return, mainly on the junk bond composition. Which overall has still played in favor of ISD.

In other words, this is the result of the compression of the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread (BAMLH0A0HYM2).

In perspective

In perspective, I do not appreciate the fact that the HY OAS is at ~320 bps when the historical average is at 527 bps. It means that on average there is a lower risk premium for this bond category. And mind you, as much as I like taking the largest positions with a contrarian view, I prefer to participate in this segment while the spread is contracting, not while it is expanding. So I was quite proud to participate in this compression and amplify my positioning during moments of spread tension, but after it reached decade lows, now it seems to have stopped contracting. Moreover, the market is going through a fairly uncertain phase, and the spread, after touching decade lows, has started to rise again, potentially signaling a trend reversal.

For this reason, personally, I exclude any form of purchase of this category, and indeed, ideologically, I think it becomes sensible to start trimming existing positions.

Risk

Well, the risk is precisely the fact that there is potentially ample room for widening in case of a downturn. And personally, I try to listen a lot to what is not being said, and some signals (interpretable) worry me quite a bit regarding the current situation. First of all, the Fed finds itself in an unpleasant situation: the shortages in the labor market must confront an inflation still today sustained at 3.01% in the USA.

Not being able to act much on the rate side, they move to the interruption of QT, and many banks seem to be refueling from the interbank market. In the meantime, the divergence between the LEI index and the CEI index is widening, a signal that yes, there is an expansion, but that expectations are not then so coherent, especially highlighted by the shortages of future orders in the manufacturing sector. Elements that make one think of increases in the future default rate. And even if on ISD, a slight increase in defaults would theoretically not have such a devastating impact, since inside there are more than 800 securities … I also think that the market would not appreciate it, and the sell off would be negative in capital value on the underlying. Or at least, this is what history teaches (even recent, see April 2025).

Conclusion

To conclude, I see quality in this CEF and an interesting weighting among the distributions, resulting in a competitive fund on the market, especially during the compression of the HY OAS. Moving to the context in which it is placed, I have given you my opinion: with a spread still below the average, which seems to be reversing course, and the uncertainty in the market at this moment, I think that any purchases should be postponed, which is why the right rating for me is HOLD.

data.title: Here's Why I Think Salesforce Is One Of The Better AI Investments
data.description:
Thesis: Salesforce is an attractive investment opportunity owing to AI. That said, the valuation is a touch high, and I believe the stock may slide a bit further pending Q3 2026 results due in early December. For now, I'd keep investments moderate with an eye towards the earnings release.

Is there an AI bubble? Right now, actually, I’m more concerned about the health of the larger economy in general. Still, I wouldn’t rule out an AI bubble, and at the very least, the space has gotten quite speculative. At the same time, many of the biggest players, like Microsoft and Google, are generally well-managed and certainly well-funded. Questions do persist about how exactly AI will be leveraged by both businesses and consumers. There are lots of promising applications, but in many cases, the promise is still quite a way off and rather uncertain. Further, many of the companies at the forefront of AI are focusing on fundamental technologies right now rather than real-world applications.

This makes Salesforce (CRM) especially interesting because agentic AI has pretty obvious and immediate uses for the company and its clients. Unlike many big-name AI stocks, CRM has suffered a sustained sell-off in recent months, with AI largely being viewed as a threat. There have been whispers of heightened customer attrition rates, and certainly, the risks posed by AI can’t be written off. However, I believe AI presents more upside than downside, while solid fundamentals and clear company focus should help Salesforce emerge as a winner. The risks are moderately high here, but I believe the upside is also quite high.

Execution is going to be crucial. Salesforce has been leaning into acquisitions as of late and recently acquired Informatica, which specializes in helping companies source, manage, and integrate data. When it comes to practical enterprise-level AI solutions, data is going to be crucial. At least on paper, the Informatica purchase makes a lot of sense. Salesforce’s position as a leader in CRM and business workflows makes it a strong candidate for developing practical AI agent solutions. Of course, actually delivering results is easier said than done, and competition will likely be fierce. Yet Salesforce is starting from a pretty strong starting point.

Markets in general have been having a rough go, and I don’t think the turbulence is over yet. CRM’s individual slide may not be over yet, either, but the earnings due in early December should give us insights. A soft earnings report would not surprise me. For now, I’d recommend keeping stakes in CRM moderate, and if and when CRM does bottom out, I believe the overall AI skepticism the company has faced will make a turnaround rather slow, so there’s no need to rush into investing.

Agentic AI Offers Perhaps The Most Attractive Near-Term AI Opportunities

How exactly will AI make money? While ideas are emerging and solutions are being implemented, this field is so nascent that it’s hard to predict how the technology will develop. That said, at least in the near-term, agentic AI offers some of the best opportunities, and Salesforce is in a strong position to work with existing clients and processes to launch agentic AI tools that deliver value. Agentic AI systems are designed to perform complex tasks without needing much human intervention or oversight. A simple AI-empowered chatbot that fetches data for customers and answers questions is one type of agentic AI, but the agents can be designed to accomplish far more complex tasks.

Salesforce recently announced that its acquiring Spindle AI (with the deal expected to close in Q4 2024). Spindle AI allows CFOs and others to quickly and easily plot out “what if” scenarios. For example, if you’re sourcing AI hardware and want to see the impact of tariffs in different scenarios based on who you’re sourcing from, Spindle AI’s agents can help you do that in seconds. Business planning often involves plotting out a select number of scenarios, but Spindle AI can create scenario landscapes that show not just a limited number of scenarios, but all possible scenarios.

Crucially, a powerful AI system can make decisions on its own and execute tasks without oversight. They can also learn on the job and adapt from interactions. In a sense, a powerful AI agent can be thought of as a digital employee. While chatbots have to follow a limited script, AI agents are more dynamic and flexible. This makes them more effective for handling both simple and complex tasks.

One major issue with Large Language Models (LLMs), and especially the popular consumer-oriented ones, is hallucination, or essentially, outputting incorrect results. This could be due to biases or deficiencies in training, incorrect data, or poorly modeled assumptions, among other things. Data is particularly crucial, and Salesforce is proactively looking to reduce hallucination by ensuring accurate data. Informatica specializes in data, and Spindle AI claims that its AI agents are no more subject to hallucination than human experts.

There are worries that AI will take Salesforce’s lunch, so to speak, by allowing companies to develop more easily in-house solutions to replace CRM's offerings. However, an MIT study found that 95% of generative AI projects have failed to produce a return. Internally developed projects were the most likely to fail, while collaborations with vendors and domain experts (Salesforce is both) have enjoyed much higher success rates. I suspect we’ll find many companies throwing in the towel on internal AI projects, instead opting to work with external vendors, like CRM.

CRM’s Long Slide Has Improved Valuation Metrics and Fundamentals

AI stocks are becoming quite expensive. Nvidia’s GAAP and non-GAAP (TTM) PE ratios both weigh in at about 51. Microsoft (MSFT) is much cheaper with both PE ratios weighing in at about 35. Google’s PE ratios look downright cheap in comparison at about 28, and Google remains my favorite AI and Magnificent 7 stock, for now.

Still, diversification is good, and CRM increasingly looks like an attractive bet. CRM’s GAAP (TTM) PE ratio is a bit high at 33.93, but the non-GAAP (TTM) PE ratio is much more attractive at 21.86. According to CRM’s Q2 2026 earnings presentation, the non-GAAP results exclude the amortization of purchased intangibles and stock-based compensation. The valuation here is still a bit expensive. I’ve been keeping a casual eye on CRM, but the high valuation has kept me away. CRM has the best PS ratio of the stocks considered here at 5.66 to MSFT’s 12.49, GOOG’s 8.96, and NVDA’s 26.85. Especially amid high CapEx spending and other trends, I find myself paying increasingly close attention to PS ratios, particularly with AI stocks, so the comparatively low PS ratio is nice to see.

Revenue growth for business SaaS is solid at 8.33% YOY. That said, AI companies are enjoying far stronger revenue growth, as shown below. This relatively slow growth is a concern. In their Q2 2026 investor presentation, the company mentions 12,500 closed Agentforce deals, but I wonder how many of them are driving serious revenue. CRM’s gross margins are excellent, but as seen in the next image below, its net income margin is much lower.

Salesforce is known for being pretty aggressive in the acquisition space. I expected to find relatively high debt levels and little cash, but by and large, the company is in good financial shape. Keep in mind, however, that some key acquisitions have not been closed yet and could impact the company’s finances. Anyway, CRM has $15.37 billion in cash and short-term investments, $5.6 billion in accounts receivable, and $97.5 billion in assets. This compares to $22.5 billion in current liabilities and $36.25 billion in total liabilities.

Ultimately, I do find CRM a bit expensive. If I do pick up a stake, I will keep it moderate for now and wait to see how the Q3 report due on December 3rd looks. If CRM outperforms, I’ll probably regret this position, but while I think we are nearing the bottom, I’m not convinced we’re there just yet. My fellow analysts assign CRM a buy rating, but Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give a hold rating, with valuation getting poor marks. I fall in between, more or less, but agree that the valuation is a bit high right now.

Where I Could Be Wrong

With my long-term bullish sentiments, it must be noted that CRM is working in a competitive space, and while so far the company seems to be focused more on collaboration with AI companies like ChatGPT, these more pure AI companies could decide to take on Salesforce more directly. Execution will not be easy either.

If Salesforce’s Q3 report widely exceeds expectations, I may regret my near-term bearish leanings. AI is becoming a bit of a buzzword, and in the short term, that will probably equate to companies making large investments in AI. This could make Salesforce a natural partner, and they may be moving more quickly than I have so far recognized.

Key Takeaway: A Soft Buy For Now As We Wait For Q3 Results

CRM is a stock I’ve been keeping a casual eye on for some time. Going forward, it’s going to be one of the companies I pay close attention to. There is a lot of potential here, even if the path forward is likely to prove arduous. AI may be a bit bubbly in some areas, but I think CRM has a credible chance of tapping into major tailwinds. For now, my focus will be on the next earnings report.

data.title: When Investors Panic, I Buy Eli Lilly
data.description:
Thesis Summary

In Q3 2025, Eli Lilly (LLY) delivered another blockbuster quarter with 54% revenue growth, massive operating leverage, and a further raise to full-year guidance.

This is a true haven right now, as the AI narrative begins to unwind and investors begin to seek safety.

The incretin franchise, Zepbound, Mounjaro, and soon, orforglipron, is performing well.

Beyond obesity and diabetes, Lilly also expanded its already deep pipeline with a $2.6B bispecific antibody deal with ABL Bio and multiple late-stage wins in oncology, neuroscience, and immunology.

Given its robust cash generation, global scale, manufacturing expansion, and strong pricing power, Lilly’s bull case becomes overwhelming.

Although I recently favoured Novo (NVO) over Lilly, this is also a great time to be a Lilly investor.

Lilly Q3

Lilly posted another phenomenal quarter, with performance that would be impressive in any market environment.

Revenue reached $17.6B up 54% YoY, and the company returned $2 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks,

The company continued to invest aggressively in R&D and its portfolio of products.

Gross margin reached 83.6% and performance margin came in at 48.3%, up 8.3 pts YoY. The company also raised FY25 guidance to $63 billion in revenues and around 23$ in EPS

The core growth engine continues to be the Obesity and Diabetes drugs, with strong international uptake following launch in over 55 countries.

Unsurprisingly, the stock has rallied strongly over the last month and has held up well during this market sell-off.

Still A Great Time To Buy

But even with Lilly at all-time highs, I’d argue this is now the most compelling time to buy in recent years.

Let’s break it down.

A Bear-Market-Proof Business Model

Even if markets roll over, Lilly’s fundamentals won’t

The demand for obesity and diabetes drugs is not cyclical. Moreover, insurance coverage remains a multi-year expansion story.

Cash-pay channels like LillyDirect and other telehealth options are accelerating adoption independent of economic conditions.

And to top things off, international markets continue seeing massive out-of-pocket growth.

At a time when investors are rotating out of overhyped AI companies and unprofitable speculative names, Lilly stands as one of the best defensive companies with strong cash flows and margins.

Orforglipron And A Strong Pipeline

Lilly’s pipeline continues to expand well beyond its current incretin dominance, led by orforglipron, the company’s upcoming oral GLP-1 and arguably its most important long-term catalyst.

With six positive Phase 3 trials, a complete regulatory package for obesity, and a U.S. launch expected next year, orforglipron offers injectable-level efficacy in a simple daily pill.

And what’s more, Lilly can manufacture the drug on an enormous scale, with billions of doses already produced and multiple new U.S. facilities being built.

Beyond orforglipron, Lilly is advancing retatrutide, a next-generation triple agonist targeting deeper weight loss for high-BMI patients, with up to six Phase 3 readouts expected by 2026.

The broader pipeline also remains strong, spanning oncology, Alzheimer’s, Lp(a) reduction, and new neuro/immunology programs.

Management has stated that orforglipron could reach tens of millions of patients globally, and they are building the manufacturing muscle to support that vision.

This is likely the most important obesity drug launch since Wegovy and Zepbound.

Durable Moat

Lilly is executing a manufacturing expansion that few competitors can match:

  • New Virginia API facility

  • New Texas small-molecule plant

  • Puerto Rico expansion

  • Two more US facility announcements are coming

  • Billions of orforglipron doses already stockpiled

These investments the company is making today will set the company apart tomorrow.

Valuation

In spite of the recent rally, Lilly still looks cheap where it matters.

Both in terms of PEG and forward PEG, the company is attractively priced. Earnings are expected to grow at over 20% CAGR over the coming years, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see the company keep beating expectations.

Risks

Investing in Lilly is not without risks.

Although we have some more clarity, regulation could still be a headwind for GLP-1s. Be it changes in the patent or the legality of compounded versions, it’s unclear just how much competition Lilly will face in the coming years.

Management has emphasized that Lilly is best positioned when pricing aligns with clinical value, but policy risk is still real.

Also, while supply constraints have eased, global demand for incretins is unprecedented, and any manufacturing bottlenecks, particularly for injectables, could affect short-term results.

There is also execution risk across Lilly’s expanding pipeline. The new ABL Bio bispecific program is early-stage and carries high clinical uncertainty, and key Alzheimer’s trials could still go either way.

Final Thoughts

Eli Lilly remains one of the clearest market-beating stories in public equities.

For investors navigating a turbulent macro backdrop, Lilly offers something incredibly rare. A secular, durable growth engine capable of compounding through any market cycle.

In a bear market, that’s exactly the kind of company you want to own.

data.title: From Alternative To Essential: The Expanding Role Of Private Market Investing
data.description:
Not long ago, private markets sat on the fringes of most portfolios – niche strategies used to pick up extra yield around the edges of traditional portfolios. That’s no longer the case. Over the past two decades, private markets have moved steadily into the mainstream, propelled by strong performance relative to public markets, a broader opportunity set, and greater accessibility for investors of all sizes. What was once considered an alternative is now becoming essential.

This evolution isn’t just about performance; it is also about relevance. The investment landscape is shifting under the weight of powerful forces: aging demographics, rapid digitization, and the global drive to decarbonize. Each of these trends is creating new capital needs and, in turn, new opportunities for private lenders and investors willing to take a longer-term view.

With more than 60 years of experience investing in private markets, Principal Asset Management has observed how quickly the definition of “core” continues to evolve. The expanding opportunity set across private real estate, infrastructure debt, investment-grade private credit, and direct lending underscores a growing need for more flexible, durable sources of return. As investors seek steadier income and growth in a shifting market environment, private markets are increasingly positioned at the center of long-term portfolio construction.

Private Real Estate

From reset to reinvention

The commercial real estate (CRE) market has undergone a significant reset and now appears positioned for renewed growth. Valuations have declined, in aggregate, by roughly 20–25% over the past two years, leaving sentiment cautious but improving. Historical patterns suggest this cycle is following a familiar trajectory: public markets have already stabilized, and private valuations are beginning to recover, with several CRE segments posting consecutive quarters of positive returns.

As the market resets, investors are shifting back toward fundamentals, focusing on property-level cash flows, balance sheet discipline, and regional supply-demand dynamics. The perception of CRE as primarily office, retail, and multifamily assets no longer fits today’s landscape. Instead, CRE has evolved into a $25 trillion ecosystem spanning 18 subsectors, many of which are linked to long-term demographic and technological shifts.

Changing composition and structural growth drivers

The modern CRE market increasingly reflects the needs of a changing economy, with new sectors emerging as key sources of growth. Data centers and senior housing have become significant components of the investable universe, benefiting from both demographic and technological forces. The rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) is intensifying demand for power infrastructure and purpose-built data facilities, while an aging population is driving sustained investment in senior and assisted living communities. Together, these trends are reshaping CRE strategies, pushing investors toward assets that align with digital transformation, the energy transition, and evolving societal needs rather than traditional property types alone. As a point of reference, nearly 70% of listed REIT market cap is now concentrated in “next generation” property types compared to ~40% for the private real estate market.

Subsector insights and emerging themes

Rental housing: Headlines often cite a housing shortage of millions of units, and while the underproduction is real, the framing is incomplete. The more pressing challenge is a mismatch between the type and location of housing and where demand actually exists. Rentership remains a cornerstone of the housing market, accounting for approximately 35% of households. But apartments are just one part of the story. Single-family rentals, manufactured housing, seniors housing, and even student housing are all meaningful and growing segments of the rental landscape. A holistic investment approach that spans geographies and the full spectrum of rental housing is needed in this market.

Office market: The post-pandemic correction in the office sector remains pronounced. Average-quality properties across the top 50 markets have seen valuations fall more than 60% from their peaks, while higher-quality properties owned by listed REITs are down 36%. However, according to JLL, roughly 90% of U.S. office vacancies are concentrated in just 30% of properties, and largely in older, less competitive buildings. In contrast, modern, energy-efficient buildings in prime locations continue to attract tenants and remain relatively resilient. Indeed, while private market office valuations for average-quality buildings across the top 50 markets sit at their lows overall, 19 of these markets are now seeing valuations rise. U.S. public REITs, which are often a leading indicator, have already rebounded 60% from their October 2023 lows. As capital re-enters the sector, selective lending or even opportunistic acquisitions of high-quality, well-located assets may present compelling value in the private real estate space.

Data centers: Data centers sit at the intersection of real estate and infrastructure—and increasingly form part of “digital real assets” strategies that span both domains. Why might data centers be viewed as infrastructure? They share many defining traits with traditional infrastructure assets such as toll roads, utilities, and airports:

  • Essential service function
  • Long-term, contracted revenue streams
  • High barriers to entry
  • Structural demand growth
  • Leases are tied to power availability, instead of the size of the usage area

These features support stable, long-term cash flows, relative insulation from economic cycles, and strong secular growth drivers, chiefly cloud adoption, AI adoption, and the exponential rise in digital data. However, despite their strong historical performance in both public and private markets, data centers remain underrepresented in many institutional real estate portfolios.

Positioning and portfolio implications

While some investors remain cautious, CRE has already absorbed its repricing and is now entering a more constructive phase, with valuations rising to varying degrees. The asset class has become less cyclical with the emergence of alternative property types, like data centers, with secular growth drivers. However, understanding the interaction between public and private markets, as well as debt and equity, remains essential. For many institutional portfolios, CRE’s hybrid characteristics, combining income-like stability with equity-like upside, make it a compelling diversifier.

As valuations start to recover after a 2-year correction and new growth areas emerge, investors who focus on strong fundamentals and disciplined selection are likely to uncover opportunities that combine steady income, moderate growth potential, and durable long-term value.

The takeaway

Commercial real estate is emerging from a broad valuation reset with more attractive pricing, stronger underwriting discipline, and a wider range of investable opportunities. While challenges persist in specific sectors such as office, the broader market’s structural evolution, toward data, housing, and social infrastructure, supports a more constructive outlook. For investors, CRE offers renewed relevance as a source of differentiated yield and portfolio diversification amid shifting macro and capital market conditions.

Private Infrastructure Debt

Stable and predictable cash flows—resilient, essential hard assets

Few asset classes embody durability as clearly as private infrastructure. Its long record of performance—through the dot-com bust, the Global Financial Crisis, commodity shock events, and even the pandemic’s severe disruption to global transportation—underscores its resilience. In each downturn, the asset class has not only recovered but also evolved to meet new economic realities. That strength is rooted in the essential nature of the underlying assets: ports, airports, roads, rail, utilities, power, energy, digital networks, and other social assets that underpin both local and global economies. The contractual and regulated nature of these assets contributes to consistently low default rates and stable income streams, a combination that long-duration investors find especially compelling.

A diverse and evolving opportunity set

Principal’s private infrastructure strategy comprises five broad categories: power, energy, transportation, digital, and social, each with distinct themes and growth drivers. Transportation often serves as the anchor exposure, complemented by strategic allocations to areas such as power generation, energy, digital, and social infrastructure.

Managers with expertise across both real estate and infrastructure are particularly well positioned to capture opportunities in social infrastructure, which includes healthcare, housing, and education, and in digital infrastructure, where data centers have matured into an asset class of their own. This cross-sector approach allows investors to balance stable, largely contractual cash flows with exposure to growth-oriented segments of the economy.

Secular tailwinds and long-term growth drivers

Looking forward, private infrastructure stands to benefit from powerful structural shifts already reshaping global markets:

  • The energy transition demands massive investment in power and grid modernization.
  • The growth of AI and digitalization is fueling demand for data centers, fiber networks, and reliable power sources.
  • Demographic shifts and aging populations are expanding investment needs in housing, including long-term care facilities and other social infrastructure.

The intersection between the energy transition and digital expansion is especially notable. As economies electrify and digitize simultaneously, new projects will emerge to reinforce aging power grids, expand power and transmission capacity, enhance grid reliability, and address water infrastructure needs—areas that continue to attract long-term capital.

The need for infrastructure investments is global. Europe, for example, continues to advance energy transition mandates with greater EV adoption and ongoing decarbonization efforts, creating investment potential in transportation electrification and power and energy.

Risk, liquidity, and the role of infrastructure debt

While the opportunity set is broad, investors must navigate familiar challenges, including potential for policy uncertainty and regulatory changes. Illiquidity also remains a key consideration from a portfolio construction lens. Private infrastructure benefits from a small but growing secondary ‘by appointment’ market, providing investors with some exit options.

Infrastructure debt, in particular, offers an attractive balance of risk and return. With stable and predictable cash flows, it provides defensive characteristics in uncertain environments and serves as a resilient portfolio through economic cycles. As a result, it can serve both a stabilizing role in diversified portfolios and a strategic role in meeting long-term income and liability objectives.

High yield infrastructure debt extends these advantages. It offers investors access to the same essential, often hard-asset sectors, but with enhanced yields to compensate for complexity or structural considerations. As such, it can serve as a complement to both investment-grade infrastructure debt and traditional high yield credit, delivering income with greater resilience for tailored portfolio solutions.

The takeaway

Private infrastructure continues to demonstrate its relevance as a long-duration, income-generating asset class with stable fundamentals and strong structural support. High yield infrastructure debt also provides complementary portfolio construction solutions to other high yield fixed income products. While policy uncertainty and illiquidity require careful management, the combination of predictable cash flows, hard assets and collateral, and yield premiums above public alternatives positions the asset class as a durable component in portfolios focused on capital preservation and steady income generation.

Investment-Grade Private Credit

Customized capital in a public-grade world

Investment-grade (IG) private credit occupies a unique space in private markets, offering investors the stability of high-quality borrowers with the structural advantages of privately negotiated terms. It’s a corner of the credit market that rarely makes headlines, precisely because it delivers what investors value most: steady performance and limited volatility. Yet even this relatively insulated segment has evolved, shaped by shifts in regulation, borrowing patterns, and the growing role of nonbank lenders.

A steady source of incremental return

For investors, IG private credit provides a way to deploy a portion of low-risk capital toward higher-yielding opportunities than those available in public fixed income. The asset class has become increasingly favored for its focus on security selection, strong structural protections, and relative resilience in volatile markets. Private placements, in particular, enable investors to capture yield premiums while maintaining exposure to investment-grade credits.

Illiquidity remains a key consideration. However, most transactions in this space carry ratings that meet institutional mandates, and the higher compensation for reduced liquidity often makes the trade-off worthwhile. The result is a segment that offers attractive income with less downside risk than comparably rated public bonds, especially in periods of widening credit spreads.

Structural strength and covenant protection

One of the defining features of IG private credit is its embedded covenant protection. These covenants help safeguard investors in the event of financial stress and allow them to take proactive measures long before a borrower defaults. Combined with the ability to sell positions in a modest but functioning secondary market, these features make private placements a reliable and flexible component of a defensive portfolio.

Emerging opportunities and challenges

Within the market, the asset-backed securities (ABS) segment offers a growing area of opportunity where investors can earn meaningful premiums, particularly in manager- or NAV-based lending structures.
